petitioner_background: The petitioner seeks classification of the beneficiary as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, specifically as a lighting designer. The beneficiary had previously been approved for O-1 nonimmigrant classification as an alien with extraordinary ability. According to the record, the beneficiary worked as a lighting director and designer for 25 years, providing lighting for various performers and shows including the Backstreet Boys, Sylvain Cossette, Lara Fabian, Tap Dogs, Cirque du Soleil, Wonderful Town, and opening acts for Aerosmith, Bryan Adams, Pearl Jam, and Madonna. The petition was filed on August 23, 2007.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed the beneficiary received two Felix Awards as Lighting Designer of the Year in 1996 and 2003 from ADISQ (Association du disque, de l'industrie de spectacle quebecois et de la video). Evidence submitted included: (1) copies of letters from ADISQ dated November 20, 1996 and November 3, 2003 congratulating the beneficiary; (2) a copy of a 1997 Montreal journal 'Spectacles' mentioning the beneficiary's receipt of the Felix award; (3) a January 12, 2004 letter from a partner with Phaneuf stating the beneficiary was 'recently awarded the FELIX Award in Montreal, Canada' and claiming it was 'the most prestigious industry award in this market for lighting designers'; (4) an article about ADISQ indicating the Felix awards were implemented in 1979 to honor Quebec musicians/music professionals dissatisfied with rarely being nominated for Juno awards, and alleging television ratings reached approximately two million viewers; (5) information purportedly from Wikipedia; and (6) a copy of the 'rules and regulation' for the Felix award adopted by ADISQ on February 28, 2007, which required candidates be Canadian citizens residing in Quebec or have an enterprise with a Quebec head office. Counsel compared television coverage of Felix awards (2 million viewers, ~6% of Canadian population) to the 2008 Grammy Awards (17.5 million viewers).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient for multiple reasons. First, the translations of the ADISQ letters and Spectacles journal did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) because they did not identify the translator or contain certifications of competency and completeness, so the evidence was not probative. Second, the letter from the Phaneuf partner did not indicate the basis of his knowledge of the beneficiary's award, and nothing in the record corroborated his statement regarding the prestige of the Felix awards. Third, the ADISQ article did not indicate where it was published, its bibliography cited only ADISQ sources, and the authors provided no reference supporting the television ratings claim. Fourth, Wikipedia information was not accorded weight because there are no assurances about the reliability of content from this open, user-edited site, citing Lamilem Badasa v. Mukasey, 540 F.3d 909. Fifth, the rules document showed the award was restricted to Quebec residents/enterprises. The AAO found no evidence showing the Felix awards command significant recognition beyond the events in which they are presented, no evidence winners were announced in major media, and counsel's assertions about viewership percentages were not corroborated. The AAO also noted that beneficiary's receipt of the award itself had not been established.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) articles from Spectacles, Le Journal de Quebec, and La Presse; (2) an April 15, 1998 press release from Martin Professional about a Backstreet Boys tour featuring lighting from Martin Professional, identifying the beneficiary as lighting director; (3) a May 17, 2007 press release from Martin featuring the beneficiary's use of the company's equipment on tour with the musical Wonderful Town; (4) documentation about performances of Sylvain Cossette, appearing to be retrieved from the Martin Professional website; (5) documentation discussing a Backstreet Boys performance at the NEC Arena in Birmingham discussing the beneficiary's role in lighting the tour; and (6) Wikipedia documentation about Le Journal de Montreal, La Presse, Le Nouvelliste and Le Journal de Quebec. Counsel asserted Le Journal de Montreal is 'the largest French-language newspaper in North America' and Le Journal de Quebec is 'the newspaper with the highest circulation of any city in Quebec.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient for several reasons. The translations of the Spectacles, Le Journal de Quebec, and La Presse articles did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) — they were only partial translations, the translator was not identified, and there was no certification of competency or accuracy. Many documents did not identify the media in which they were published as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The Martin Professional press releases were company self-promotion about the company's products and equipment, not primarily about the beneficiary, and were not evidence that an independent journalist found the alien worthy of coverage; no documentation showed the press releases were actually printed in any media. The Sylvain Cossette documentation was almost illegible and appeared to be about the equipment used, not the beneficiary. The NEC Arena document did not include title, date, and author as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). Nothing in the submitted Martin documentation indicated Martin Professional was a magazine or any type of media. Wikipedia information was not reliable and would not be accorded weight. The AAO noted none of the documentation was dated less than five years prior to filing. Counsel's unsupported assertions about circulation did not constitute evidence, citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. The AAO held that to qualify as major media, publications should have significant national distribution in a predominant language.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters of recommendation from several individuals including: (1) a March 19, 2004 letter from the vice-president of Soltech, Inc. stating the petitioner 'is an expert in the lighting industry' and 'is a conscientious, efficient, organized and a highly skilled lighting designer with special skills of expertise in theatrical events'; (2) a letter from the president of Gestion Serge Brouillette Inc. stating the beneficiary's 'organization, artistic and creative skills have allowed him to design wonderful lighting for all of our productions' and that his 'lighting designs are forever evolving with the technical advances within his field'; and (3) an unsigned January 26, 2004 letter from Entertainment Design describing the beneficiary as 'a professional designer of the highest caliber who carefully researches, plans and prepares his productions.' Counsel pointed to references to the beneficiary's 'creative skills,' 'unique techniques,' and innovativeness.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that none of the authors stated that the beneficiary made an original contribution of major significance to the field. The letters described the beneficiary as skilled, creative, and unique, but none of this rose to the level of making a contribution of major significance to the field of endeavor. Counsel took issue with the director's requirement that contributions have 'demonstrable influence on the field or set a standard for which others aspire,' but pointed to no specific achievements constituting an original contribution of major significance. The AAO concluded that a general analysis that the beneficiary is 'creative' or 'unique' is not the equivalent of establishing that the beneficiary has made a contribution of major significance to his field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed the beneficiary met this criterion based on his position as lighting director for several performers, including the Backstreet Boys, Sylvain Cossette, Lara Fabian, and for opening acts for Aerosmith, Bryan Adams, Pearl Jam, and Madonna. The petitioner also stated the beneficiary worked for Claude Dubois and Stephane Rousseau (allegedly winning a Felix Award for his work for them), was the lighting director for Tap Dogs, and worked with Cirque du Soleil, Franco Dragone, and Wonderful Town.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the plain language of this criterion reveals it relates to the visual arts. It is inherent in the beneficiary's job that he provides lighting for performing artists, so not every production in which he provides lighting is a showcase or exhibition of his work. Duties or activities which nominally fall under a given regulatory criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) do not demonstrate national or international acclaim if they are inherent or routine in the occupation itself. Without evidence that the beneficiary's services were comparable to the exclusive artistic showcases that might serve to meet this criterion for a visual artist, the AAO could not conclude the beneficiary met this criterion. The AAO noted the petitioner's work was more relevant to the 'leading or critical role' criterion and rejected counsel's argument that this interpretation would bar any artist whose art enhances another's performance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel's August 20, 2007 letter stated the beneficiary had worked as a lighting director and director for singers, shows and entertainment tours for 25 years, 'many of which are internationally renowned.' The beneficiary's resume listed the entertainment personnel and shows on which he had worked as a lighting designer and director, including the Backstreet Boys, Sylvain Cossette, Lara Fabian, Tap Dogs, Cirque du Soleil, Franco Dragone, Wonderful Town, Claude Dubois, and Stephane Rousseau. Supporting documentation about the renown of these acts was largely retrieved from Wikipedia, including information about Wonderful Town, Lara Fabian, the Backstreet Boys, Claude Dubois and Stephane Rousseau.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the beneficiary's role as lighting director and designer for the various acts and shows was in a critical role. However, the record did not establish that any of the singers, shows or entertainment tours on which he worked enjoyed a distinguished reputation. Most of the supporting documentation came from Wikipedia, which was unreliable and would not be afforded any weight. Other documentation did not indicate the source. While some acts like the Backstreet Boys and Cirque du Soleil are internationally known, this is not the equivalent of providing evidence that the acts enjoy a distinguished reputation. The evidence was insufficient to establish that those for whom the beneficiary worked enjoyed a distinguished reputation.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to establish that the beneficiary met at least three of the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) and did not establish receipt of a major, internationally recognized award. Although the beneficiary performed a critical role as a lighting director, the petitioner failed to show the organizations had a distinguished reputation. Review of the record did not establish that the beneficiary had distinguished himself to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner did not sustain that burden. The AAO also rejected the argument that prior O-1 approval compelled approval of the I-140, noting the different regulatory standards between the two classifications.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Q Data Consulting, Inc. v. INS, 293 F. Supp. 2d 25 (D.D.C. 2003)
IKEA US v. US Dept. of Justice, 48 F. Supp. 2d 22 (D.D.C. 1999)
Fedin Brothers Co. Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989)
Texas A&M Univ. v. Upchurch, 99 Fed. Appx. 556, 2004 WL 1240482 (5th Cir. 2004)
Matter of Church Scientology International, 19 I&N Dec. 593 (Comm. 1988)
Sussex Engg. Ltd. v. Montgomery, 825 F.2d 1084 (6th Cir. 1987), cert. denied, 485 U.S. 1008 (1988)
Louisiana Philharmonic Orchestra v. INS, 2000 WL 282785 (E.D. La.), aff'd, 248 F.3d 1139 (5th Cir. 2001), cert. denied, 122 S.Ct. 51 (2001)
Buletini v. INS, 860 F. Supp. 1222 (E.D. Mich. 1994)
Lamilem Badasa v. Michael Mukasey, 540 F.3d 909 (8th Cir. 2008)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner submitted multiple foreign-language documents (ADISQ letters, Spectacles journal, Le Journal de Quebec, La Presse articles) with translations that did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) — they did not identify the translator, did not contain certifications of translator competency, and many were only partial translations. The petitioner also submitted an unsigned January 26, 2004 letter from Entertainment Design. The purported 'rules and regulation' for the Felix award was unsigned and it was unclear whether it was the complete document. The petitioner relied heavily on Wikipedia as a source, which the AAO rejected as unreliable. Several documents failed to include required title, date, author, or identification of the publishing media.
